enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ClosersCopy is an AI copywriting platform launched in 2021 that positions itself as a long-form content and sales-copy generator rather than a general-purpose chat assistant. Its core differentiator is a library of more than 700 pre-built copywriting frameworks and templates, drawn from established direct-response and content-marketing methodologies, which users can plug their product or offer details into to produce structured drafts. The platform runs on three named AI engines: Blog AI for long-form articles and SEO content, Sales AI for persuasive sales pages, ads, and email sequences, and Story AI for narrative-driven and brand storytelling content. ClosersCopy also includes a built-in long-form document editor with tone adjustment, readability scoring, keyword research and on-page optimization tools, and inline AI suggestions that offer real-time rewrite prompts as a user types. Content generation is supported in more than 120 languages, which the vendor markets as one of the widest language ranges among AI writing tools in its category. ClosersCopy is aimed primarily at digital marketers, growth teams, copywriting agencies, e-commerce sellers, and online course creators who need to produce large volumes of sales letters, landing pages, ad copy, email campaigns, and blog drafts without hiring a dedicated copywriter for every asset. Pricing is subscription-based with three public tiers differentiated mainly by monthly generation allowance and user seats: a Starter plan around 29.99 dollars per month, a Professional plan around 49.99 dollars per month, and an Unlimited plan around 79.99 dollars per month for higher-volume users and teams. ClosersCopy previously sold lifetime-access deals through deal marketplaces, but as of 2026 those lifetime offers are no longer being sold to new customers, and the company has moved to a standard recurring-subscription model with typical money-back guarantee windows on paid plans. On review platforms such as G2, ClosersCopy holds a rating in the low-to-mid 4-out-of-5 range from roughly 1,800 reviews, with users frequently citing the framework library and pricing relative to output volume as strengths, and citing occasional inconsistency in factual accuracy and a steeper learning curve than simpler AI writing tools as drawbacks. The company appears to operate as a lean, founder-led business based in Germany rather than a large venture-backed corporation, and detailed headcount figures are not publicly disclosed. For a directory or comparison audience, the key facts to know are: ClosersCopy is a framework-driven, long-form AI copywriting tool (not primarily a chatbot or short-form social caption generator), it differentiates on breadth of proven copywriting templates and multilingual output, it is priced as an ongoing subscription starting under 30 dollars per month, and it competes most directly with other AI copywriting suites such as Copy.ai, Jasper, and Writesonic rather than with general-purpose LLM chat products.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is ClosersCopy
paragraphs: ClosersCopy is an AI writing platform focused specifically on marketing and sales copy rather than general conversation. Instead of a blank prompt box, it gives users a searchable library of more than 700 frameworks built on proven direct-response and content-marketing structures, covering formats like sales pages, cold emails, ad copy, product descriptions, and full blog posts.

The platform is organized around three purpose-built engines: Blog AI for long-form, SEO-oriented articles; Sales AI for persuasion-focused copy such as landing pages and email sequences; and Story AI for narrative and brand-storytelling content. This framework-first approach is ClosersCopy's main point of differentiation from more general AI writing assistants.

long_form_blocks.heading: ClosersCopy Pricing and Plans
paragraphs: ClosersCopy uses a tiered monthly subscription model, with the Starter plan priced around 29.99 dollars per month, a Professional plan around 49.99 dollars per month, and an Unlimited plan around 79.99 dollars per month for teams and high-volume users. Plans differ primarily in monthly generation allowance, number of user seats, and access to advanced tools like the keyword research module.

The company previously sold one-time lifetime-access licenses through third-party deal sites, which made it popular with early adopters and course-creator communities, but as of 2026 new customers are directed to the standard recurring-subscription plans rather than lifetime pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Uses ClosersCopy
paragraphs: ClosersCopy's core audience is performance marketers, digital agencies, e-commerce brands, and online course or info-product creators who need a high volume of sales-oriented copy without commissioning a copywriter for every asset. Its multilingual support, covering more than 120 languages, also makes it a common choice for teams producing localized marketing campaigns across multiple markets.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud 66 is a DevOps-as-a-Service platform that automates the build, deployment, and management of applications and databases on any public or private cloud, including AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, DigitalOcean, and Linode. Founded in 2012 by Khash Sajadi and Vic van Gool, the company is headquartered in London.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cloud 66 is a DevOps-as-a-Service platform founded in 2012 by Khash Sajadi and Vic van Gool and headquartered in London, United Kingdom. It was built to solve the problem of deploying and operating applications on raw cloud infrastructure without the cost and lock-in of a traditional Platform-as-a-Service, and without the complexity of hand-rolled configuration-management scripts. The platform lets developers connect their own cloud account, on providers including AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, DigitalOcean, and Linode, and then handles server provisioning, application deployment, and ongoing operations on top of that infrastructure. Cloud 66 originally focused heavily on Ruby on Rails applications, positioning itself as a way to bring Heroku-style simplicity to a customer's own servers, and it has since expanded to support Node.js, PHP, Python, Java, and other frameworks alongside Docker-based and Kubernetes-based deployments. Key platform capabilities include zero-downtime deployments with blue-green and canary release strategies, hosted Docker build servers with an integrated container registry supporting multiple CPU architectures, full database support including managed backups and replication for engines like PostgreSQL, MySQL, and MongoDB, autoscaling driven by real application metrics such as CPU, memory, queue backlog, and web-server response time, and automatic preview environments that spin up and tear down for git branches, tags, and pull requests. The product also includes SSL automation, custom network and firewall configuration, and a dashboard and API for managing infrastructure across multiple cloud accounts and regions from a single control plane. Cloud 66 is priced on a tiered subscription model. A free plan supports one deployment server and one static site with a capped number of daily deployments, intended for hobby projects and evaluation. Paid plans include a Developer tier around 23 dollars per month for two servers and two static sites, a Team tier around 47 dollars per month (when billed annually) for four servers and five static sites, and a Business tier around 80 dollars per month for seven servers and ten static sites. An Enterprise tier with custom pricing covers larger deployments of fifty or more servers and adds dedicated account management, on-premises or dedicated deployment options, and invoicing support. Annual billing typically saves around 20 percent versus paying monthly. Add-on charges apply for extra servers, extra static sites, additional team members, extra build minutes, static-site traffic overage, and managed-backup storage overage. Cloud 66 has historically counted customers such as Adobe, the BBC, and O2 among its user base, and it competes in a category alongside platforms like Heroku, Render, Platform.sh, and self-managed Kubernetes tooling, differentiating itself by running entirely inside the customer's own cloud account rather than a shared multi-tenant platform, which gives customers direct ownership of the underlying infrastructure and avoids vendor-hosted compute lock-in.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Cloud 66
paragraphs: Cloud 66 is a DevOps-as-a-Service platform that automates deploying and operating applications on a customer's own cloud infrastructure rather than hosting workloads on a shared, vendor-owned platform. Users connect an AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, DigitalOcean, or Linode account, and Cloud 66 provisions servers, configures networking, and manages ongoing deployments, scaling, and database operations on top of it.

The platform grew out of founder Khash Sajadi's frustration with expensive, restrictive PaaS offerings and overly complex configuration-management tools like Chef, and it was originally built to bring Heroku-style simplicity to Ruby on Rails deployments on a customer's own servers before expanding to Node.js, Docker, and Kubernetes workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Deployment and Operations Features
paragraphs: Cloud 66 supports zero-downtime deployments using blue-green and canary release strategies, hosted Docker build servers with an integrated multi-architecture container registry, and full managed database support including automated backups and replication. Autoscaling can be driven by real application metrics such as CPU, memory, queue backlog, and web response time rather than simple fixed thresholds.

The platform also creates automatic preview environments for git branches, tags, and pull requests, letting teams review changes in a live environment before merging, and it provides SSL automation and custom network and firewall configuration out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cloud 66 Pricing Tiers
paragraphs: Cloud 66 offers a free plan limited to one deployment server and one static site, paid Developer, Team, and Business plans priced by the number of servers and static sites included (roughly 23, 47, and 80 dollars per month respectively), and a custom-priced Enterprise tier for organizations running fifty or more servers that adds dedicated account management and on-premises deployment options.
Annual billing offers roughly a 20 percent discount versus monthly billing, and additional servers, static sites, team members, and build minutes can be added a la carte on any paid plan.

It offers deployment across more than 70 regions on providers including AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, Scaleway, and Outscale.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cloud-IAM review: pricing, uptime SLAs, and features of this managed Keycloak identity and access management hosting service based in France.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cloud-IAM is a managed hosting service for Keycloak, the widely used open-source identity and access management (IAM) server, designed for teams that want Keycloak's single sign-on, OAuth2, and OpenID Connect capabilities without operating the underlying infrastructure themselves. The service has been running since 2018 under the tagline Keycloak Experts Since 2018, and it is delivered by a joint venture of two French technology companies, ELLIXO SAS and FGRibreau SARL, headquartered in Rennes, in the Brittany region of France. The company is small, reported at around 11 employees, but it draws on a broader engineering bench through ELLIXO, which has more than 30 engineers specialized in hosting and tuning Java-based software, giving Cloud-IAM deep operational expertise in the Java-based Keycloak stack specifically. Cloud-IAM provisions a fully dedicated Keycloak deployment per customer, rather than a shared multi-tenant instance, in the cloud region of the customer's choice, drawing on more than 70 available regions across major providers including AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, and European providers Scaleway and Outscale. This gives European customers, in particular, a way to keep identity data within the EU for GDPR compliance while still using major-cloud-grade infrastructure. Deployments are typically live within about 20 minutes of setup, and the service includes automated backups, built-in redundancy, and a 99.95 percent uptime SLA on its most popular tier, backed by 24/7 expert support from engineers focused specifically on Keycloak rather than generalist cloud support staff. Pricing is structured around the total number of user accounts provisioned in Keycloak rather than monthly active users, which the company positions as more predictable for budgeting since it does not spike with login activity. The plan structure includes a Freemium tier for up to 100 users with community support and roughly 99 percent uptime, a Starter tier and an Essential tier both beginning around 225 euros per month with community or email and ticket support respectively, a Premium tier also from around 225 euros per month that adds 24/7 on-call support and a day of included monthly consulting, and a custom-priced Max tier for the largest deployments requiring multi-region redundancy and a 30-minute incident-response commitment. All paid tiers include enterprise security certifications such as ISO 27001:2022 and SOC 2 Type 2, along with GDPR compliance documentation, which is a common purchasing requirement for the regulated industries, such as banking, healthcare, and public sector, that frequently adopt Keycloak. Cloud-IAM's core competitive position is that it removes the operational burden of self-hosting and patching Keycloak, including version upgrades, extension development, and custom domain and SSL configuration, all of which can be handled through the Cloud-IAM console without additional engineering fees on most plans. It competes with other Keycloak-hosting providers and with the alternative of self-managing Keycloak on raw cloud infrastructure or adopting a proprietary IAM product such as Auth0, Okta, or AWS Cognito instead of the open-source Keycloak project.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Cloud-IAM
paragraphs: Cloud-IAM is a managed hosting service built specifically around Keycloak, the open-source identity and access management server that provides single sign-on, OAuth2, and OpenID Connect authentication. Rather than requiring a team to install, patch, and operate Keycloak themselves, Cloud-IAM provisions a fully dedicated Keycloak instance per customer and handles the ongoing operations.

The service has run since 2018 and is delivered by a joint venture of ELLIXO SAS and FGRibreau SARL, two French companies headquartered in Rennes, France, giving it a strong European base for customers with GDPR or data-residency requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Infrastructure, Support, and Compliance
paragraphs: Cloud-IAM can deploy dedicated Keycloak instances in more than 70 regions across AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, Scaleway, and Outscale, with setup typically completing in about 20 minutes. Higher tiers carry a 99.95 percent uptime SLA and are backed by 24/7 support from engineers who specialize specifically in Keycloak rather than general cloud infrastructure.

All paid plans include ISO 27001:2022 and SOC 2 Type 2 certifications along with GDPR compliance documentation, which matters to the regulated industries, including banking and public sector organizations, that commonly rely on Keycloak for identity management.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Cloud-IAM Pricing Works
paragraphs: Cloud-IAM prices its plans based on the total number of user accounts provisioned rather than monthly active users, which the company argues produces more predictable costs. A free tier covers up to 100 users, while paid Starter, Essential, and Premium tiers begin around 225 euros per month and differ mainly in support responsiveness and SLA level, with a custom-priced Max tier for large, multi-region enterprise deployments.

enrichment.short_description: Cloud4Wi is a cloud-native, AI-powered WiFi platform that turns guest and resident WiFi networks into a customer engagement and analytics layer for physical venues such as retail stores, hospitality brands, and transit locations. The platform unifies Guest WiFi, MDU (multi-dwelling unit) WiFi, and Cloud Network Access Control on a single, hardware-independent architecture, powered by an AI engine called Hedy. Founded in February 2014 by Andrea Calcagno and Davide Quadrini, the company is now headquartered in Brooklyn, New York, after starting in Pisa, Italy.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cloud4Wi is a cloud-native WiFi and customer-engagement platform used by physical venues, including retailers, hospitality brands, cruise lines, shopping centers, and transit operators, to turn guest WiFi networks into a source of first-party customer data and real-time engagement rather than simple internet connectivity. The company was founded in February 2014 by Andrea Calcagno and Davide Quadrini, originally headquartered in Pisa, Italy, before relocating its corporate offices to San Francisco following a Series A round led by United Ventures, and later moving its corporate headquarters to New York in 2019; the company is now based in Brooklyn, New York. Cloud4Wi has raised roughly 18 million dollars in funding, including a Series B round of about 11.5 million dollars in 2017 co-led by Opus Capital and United Ventures. The platform unifies three products on one hardware-independent architecture: Guest WiFi, which delivers branded captive-portal onboarding and consent-based data capture for visitors; MDU WiFi, aimed at multi-dwelling residential properties; and Cloud Network Access Control (Cloud NAC), which extends the same architecture to secure network access use cases. All three products are powered by an AI engine called Hedy, which proactively detects network issues before they affect guests, proposes resolutions, and lets administrators ask plain-language questions to get live analytics on connections, venues, and contacts, along with ready-to-use reports and guided actions. Cloud4Wi is deliberately hardware-independent, integrating with existing access point and controller infrastructure from vendors including Cisco, Meraki, Aruba, Mist, Ubiquiti, Ruckus, and Extreme via standard protocols such as RADIUS, RadSec, 802.1X, Passpoint, and OpenRoaming, so customers can layer Cloud4Wi onto their current network estate without replacing hardware or facing firmware lock-in. The platform is SOC 2 Type II certified and is used at scale by well-known brands including Prada, MSC Cruises, Guess, and the shopping center operator Carmila; the company reports its platform is trusted across more than 70,000 locations serving over 300 million users worldwide. A publicized case with Guess describes the retailer scaling Cloud4Wi from roughly 90 European stores to more than 700 global locations, using branded WiFi onboarding to capture consent-based customer data feeding an AI-powered marketing stack, generating more than 35,000 new customer profiles per month and capturing over one million real-time interaction events annually. Pricing is public and usage-based rather than requiring a sales call for baseline tiers: each product starts around 200 dollars per month (roughly 2,400 dollars per year when billed annually), scaling by the number of access points included for Guest WiFi, personal networks for MDU WiFi, or users for Cloud NAC, with every tier including the full feature set, meaning there are no separate paid add-ons for the captive portal, the Hedy AI Engine, Passpoint and OpenRoaming support, the MyData customer portal, or CRM integrations. A 30-day free trial requiring no credit card is available for self-serve evaluation, while larger deployments above roughly 250 access points or 1,000 users move to custom Enterprise pricing negotiated with the Cloud4Wi sales team.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: CloudAMQP review: pricing, plans, and features of this managed RabbitMQ and LavinMQ hosting service from 84codes, available on AWS, GCP, and Azure.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CloudAMQP is a managed message-broker hosting service that lets development teams run RabbitMQ and LavinMQ, two open-source AMQP-compatible message queuing systems, without provisioning or operating the underlying broker infrastructure themselves. It is the flagship product of 84codes AB, a Swedish technology company founded in 2012 by Carl Horberg and headquartered in Gothenburg, Sweden, which has been recognized as one of Europe's long-term growth companies by the Financial Times. Alongside CloudAMQP, 84codes also operates CloudMQTT, a managed MQTT broker service, and develops LavinMQ itself, an in-house-built, high-performance AMQP 1.0 message broker written in the Crystal programming language and designed as a resource-efficient, high-throughput alternative to RabbitMQ. CloudAMQP automates the entire lifecycle of a message broker deployment, including setup, TLS encryption, monitoring, clustering, and automatic backups, and it is available across all major cloud platforms, spanning AWS, Google Cloud Platform, and Microsoft Azure, in 19 cloud regions globally, which lets customers co-locate their message broker in the same region and even the same virtual private cloud as their application infrastructure. Pricing is structured in two broad categories. Multi-tenant, shared-server plans are the most economical option and include a genuinely free tier for both RabbitMQ (called Little Lemur, offering 1 million messages per month, 20 connections, and 100 queues) and LavinMQ (called Loyal Lemming, offering 2 million messages per month, 40 connections, and 200 queues), plus paid shared plans (Tough Tiger for RabbitMQ and Elegant Ermine for LavinMQ) priced around 19 dollars per month with higher throughput limits. Dedicated, single-tenant plans provide guaranteed resources on their own virtual machines and range from entry-level options, such as the roughly 49 to 50 dollar per month single-node RabbitMQ or LavinMQ plans, up through high-throughput, multi-availability-zone clustered plans costing several thousand to over 17,000 dollars per month for the largest tiers, which support throughput figures ranging from a few hundred messages per second up to hundreds of thousands or over a million messages per second on the top LavinMQ tier. All plans are billed on a per-second basis, meaning the monthly invoice reflects actual instance uptime rather than a flat recurring charge regardless of usage, and optional add-ons include a dedicated Virtual Private Cloud for around 99 dollars per month and additional disk space billed at roughly 0.35 dollars per gigabyte per month. CloudAMQP publishes SOC 2 and SOC 3 compliance reports, and the service is commonly used by backend and DevOps teams to decouple microservices, handle asynchronous task queues, buffer traffic spikes, and implement event-driven architectures without the operational overhead of running RabbitMQ or LavinMQ clusters by hand. The company's exact employee headcount is not publicly disclosed, consistent with 84codes operating as a lean, engineering-focused Swedish company rather than a large, multi-department enterprise vendor.

All plans bill per second based on actual instance uptime rather than a flat monthly fee, and optional add-ons include a dedicated Virtual Private Cloud and additional disk space priced per gigabyte.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cloudbeds is a hospitality management platform founded in 2012 and headquartered in San Diego, California, built to unify the core operational systems that hotels, hostels, bed and breakfasts, and vacation rental and short-term rental operators need to run a property. Rather than selling separate point products, Cloudbeds built its property management system (PMS), channel manager, booking engine, and revenue and guest-engagement tools on a single, shared data model, meaning updates such as a new reservation or rate change propagate automatically across every connected system instead of requiring manual syncing between disconnected tools. The company operates with a fully remote workforce of more than 650 employees spread across more than 40 countries, speaking more than 30 languages internally, with shared office space available in San Diego and Sao Paulo for teams that want it. Cloudbeds reports that its platform is used by tens of thousands of properties in more than 150 countries, ranging from small independent hotels and hostels to large multi-property hotel groups and vacation rental portfolios. The current plan structure is organized into four tiers: Flex, a build-your-own-stack option that provides the core PMS and lets customers connect their existing tools through a marketplace of more than 400 third-party app integrations; One, which bundles the core PMS with distribution tools such as a channel manager, booking engine, and Google Free Booking Links; Experience, which adds guest messaging, mobile check-in, and online reputation management on top of the One package; and Enterprise, aimed at larger hotel groups and chains, which adds multi-property support, enterprise-level reporting, and a dedicated account manager. Cloudbeds also offers optional Revenue Marketing modules that can be added to any base plan for properties that want additional demand-generation and revenue-management capability. Cloudbeds does not publish flat, self-serve pricing on its website; every quote requires a conversation with the Cloudbeds sales team, and the final price depends heavily on property size (number of rooms or units), property type, location, and which plan tier and add-on modules are selected. Third-party cost-estimate sources place typical Cloudbeds pricing somewhere in the range of roughly 200 to 1,000-plus dollars per month, though actual quotes can fall outside that range for very small or very large properties. Cloudbeds' most common comparison points in the hospitality software market are other cloud PMS and hotel-tech providers such as Mews, RMS Cloud, Little Hotelier, and Guesty, and it differentiates itself primarily through its single unified data model spanning the PMS, distribution, and guest-engagement layers, plus its large third-party integration marketplace with more than 400 apps covering point-of-sale, accounting, revenue management, and other hospitality-adjacent software categories.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CloudBees is an enterprise software delivery and CI/CD company founded in 2010 by Sacha Labourey and Francois Dechery, headquartered in San Jose, California, with additional offices including Raleigh, North Carolina; Lewes, Delaware; Richmond, Virginia; Berlin; London; and Neuchatel, Switzerland. The company built its early business as the primary commercial steward and enterprise support provider for Jenkins, the widely used open-source continuous integration and continuous delivery automation server, and it has historically been closely associated with the Jenkins project and community. Over the years CloudBees expanded through acquisitions, including Codeship in 2018, Electric Cloud and Rollout in 2019, and ReleaseIQ in 2022, broadening its footprint from core CI/CD into release orchestration and DevSecOps capabilities. In 2025, CloudBees introduced CloudBees Unify as an early-access, AI-powered enterprise DevOps platform positioned as an operating layer that sits on top of a customer's existing toolchain, including Jenkins and Kubernetes, rather than requiring a full migration away from tools already in use. CloudBees Unify adds capabilities such as cross-platform DORA metrics and flow analytics for measuring delivery performance (lead time for changes, deployment frequency, change failure rate, and time to restore), Application Security Posture Management (ASPM), feature management, and release orchestration, along with an MCP-enabled AI foundation that CloudBees says supports automated pipeline intelligence; the company also announced a CloudBees Model Context Protocol server available through AWS Marketplace's AI Agents and Tools category in July 2025. Organizationally, CloudBees now presents its platform in three edition tiers that build on one another, with higher editions adding release orchestration, feature flags, compliance capabilities, and AI-driven test intelligence. On the financial and organizational side, CloudBees remains a privately held company; it closed a 150 million dollar Series F equity round together with a 95 million dollar debt facility, reaching a reported valuation of 1 billion dollars, with Delta-v Capital named among its investors. Despite that capital raise, CloudBees conducted layoffs in September 2025 affecting roughly 65 employees, or about 12 percent of its workforce, as part of a broader restructuring described publicly by CEO Anuj Kapur as part of investing in the company's future direction around CloudBees Unify. As of 2026, CloudBees employee counts across various data providers range from roughly 515 to 600, generally described as being in the 501-1,000 employee band. CloudBees does not publish detailed self-serve pricing; its pricing page directs prospects to request a custom quote based on team size, security requirements, and orchestration complexity, though third-party sources report a free plan for teams of up to 5 users and unverified estimates suggesting team-tier pricing in the range of 75 to 100 dollars per user per month, with enterprise pricing quoted individually based on user count, build capacity, and support requirements. CloudBees competes primarily with other enterprise CI/CD and DevOps platforms such as GitLab, GitHub Actions at the enterprise tier, Harness, and Atlassian's Bamboo, while retaining a distinct position as the company most closely tied to the Jenkins ecosystem specifically.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is CloudBees
paragraphs: CloudBees is an enterprise CI/CD and software delivery company founded in 2010 by Sacha Labourey and Francois Dechery, headquartered in San Jose, California. It built its original business as the leading commercial company behind Jenkins, the open-source continuous integration and delivery automation server widely used across the software industry.

Through acquisitions including Codeship, Electric Cloud, Rollout, and ReleaseIQ, CloudBees expanded from core CI/CD into release orchestration, feature management, and DevSecOps, and in 2025 it introduced CloudBees Unify, an AI-powered enterprise DevOps platform designed to sit on top of a customer's existing toolchain, including Jenkins and Kubernetes, without requiring full migration.

long_form_blocks.heading: CloudBees Unify Features
paragraphs: CloudBees Unify adds cross-platform DORA metrics and flow analytics for tracking delivery performance, Application Security Posture Management, feature management, and release orchestration on top of a customer's existing tools. It also includes an MCP-enabled AI foundation and a CloudBees Model Context Protocol server, which the company launched on AWS Marketplace in July 2025 to support AI-driven pipeline automation.

The platform is organized into three editions that build on one another, with higher editions adding compliance capabilities and AI-driven test intelligence on top of core CI/CD and orchestration features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Recent Company Status (2025-2026)
paragraphs: CloudBees remains privately held, having closed a 150 million dollar Series F equity round alongside a 95 million dollar debt facility at a reported 1 billion dollar valuation, with Delta-v Capital among its investors. Despite this growth financing, the company carried out layoffs in September 2025 affecting roughly 65 employees, about 12 percent of its workforce, as part of a restructuring tied to its CloudBees Unify strategy under CEO Anuj Kapur.

As of 2026, employee counts across data providers range from roughly 515 to 600, generally placing CloudBees in the 501-1,000 employee band, with additional offices in Raleigh, Lewes, Richmond, Berlin, London, and Neuchatel.

enrichment.short_description: Cloudcraft is a cloud architecture diagramming tool that automatically generates live, editable diagrams of AWS, Azure, Google Cloud, Oracle Cloud, and vSphere environments by reading a customer's live infrastructure data rather than requiring diagrams to be drawn entirely by hand. It is headquartered in New York City.

enrichment.seo_title: Cloudcraft Review: AWS Diagramming Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cloudcraft review: pricing, live AWS and Azure diagramming features, and how this Datadog-owned architecture tool works in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cloudcraft is a cloud infrastructure diagramming tool founded in 2015 and headquartered in New York City, built around the idea that architecture diagrams should stay automatically synchronized with a customer's real, live cloud infrastructure rather than becoming outdated the moment someone draws them by hand. Instead of manually placing shapes on a canvas, Cloudcraft can connect to a customer's cloud account and reverse-engineer a diagram of the actual deployed resources and their relationships, then keep that diagram updated automatically as the underlying infrastructure changes. Datadog, the New York-based observability and security monitoring company, acquired Cloudcraft in a deal announced on November 3, 2022; despite some sources loosely associating the acquisition with an earlier year, the transaction was confirmed by Datadog's own press release and investor materials as a November 2022 acquisition, and the deal terms were not disclosed. Since the acquisition, Cloudcraft has continued to operate as both a standalone product, available directly at cloudcraft.co with its own documentation, and as an integrated feature inside the broader Datadog platform, documented separately as Cloudcraft in Datadog, which combines live architecture visualization with Datadog's monitoring, security, and cost-management capabilities. Under Datadog's ownership, Cloudcraft's cloud coverage has expanded well beyond its original AWS-only focus to also support Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, and VMware vSphere environments, letting teams visualize multi-cloud and hybrid infrastructure in one tool. The product renders diagrams in a distinctive isometric, 3D-style visual format rather than flat boxes-and-lines diagrams, which has been one of its recognizable design signatures since before the acquisition, and it supports drag-and-drop manual editing on top of the auto-generated baseline, along with cost estimation and budget-tracking overlays that show the estimated monthly cost of resources directly on the diagram. Cloudcraft's pricing follows a freemium model: a Free plan lets individual users create AWS and Azure architecture diagrams for both commercial and non-commercial projects at no cost, though live-sync and advanced features are typically reserved for paid tiers. The Pro plan is priced at roughly 40.83 dollars per user per month when billed annually, or 99 dollars per user per month when billed monthly, and includes a 14-day free trial that does not require a credit card, plus a 30-day money-back guarantee on top of that trial. An Enterprise plan is priced at roughly 100 dollars per user per month billed annually, or 120 dollars per user per month billed monthly, and adds enterprise-oriented capabilities such as expanded collaboration, administration, and support. As a small, focused product team of roughly a dozen to several dozen people operating within the larger Datadog organization, Cloudcraft's team size is modest, but customers benefit from the financial backing and platform integration of its much larger parent company. Cloudcraft is most commonly compared to other cloud diagramming and visualization tools such as Lucidchart, diagrams.net (draw.io), and native AWS architecture icon sets, but its core differentiator remains the live-data-driven, automatically generated diagram rather than a purely manual drawing canvas.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Cloudcraft
paragraphs: Cloudcraft is a cloud architecture diagramming tool that connects to a customer's live cloud account and automatically generates an up-to-date diagram of the deployed resources and how they relate to each other, rather than requiring diagrams to be drawn entirely from scratch. It renders diagrams in a distinctive isometric, 3D-style visual style that has become one of its recognizable characteristics.

The company was founded in 2015 and is headquartered in New York City. Its original focus was AWS architecture diagrams, and it has since expanded to also support Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, and VMware vSphere environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Datadog Acquisition
paragraphs: Datadog acquired Cloudcraft in a deal announced on November 3, 2022, with financial terms not disclosed. This is a more recent transaction than some secondary sources suggest, and the correct acquisition year, according to Datadog's own press release and investor announcement, is 2022 rather than 2020.

Since the acquisition, Cloudcraft continues to operate both as a standalone product at cloudcraft.co and as an integrated feature inside the Datadog platform, where its live architecture diagrams can be combined with Datadog's broader monitoring, security, and cost-management tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Cloudcraft Pricing and Plans
paragraphs: Cloudcraft offers a Free plan for individuals creating AWS and Azure diagrams, a Pro plan priced around 40.83 dollars per user per month billed annually (99 dollars per month billed monthly) with a 14-day free trial and 30-day money-back guarantee, and an Enterprise plan around 100 dollars per user per month billed annually (120 dollars per month billed monthly) for larger teams needing expanded administration and support.
